Portugal is officially known as the Portuguese Republic.
Portugal is located in the Iberian Peninsula in Southwestern Europe.
Lisbon is the capital and largest city of Portugal.
Earliest signs of settlement in the western part of the Iberian Peninsula date to 5500 BC.
Celtic and Iberian peoples arrived in the first millennium BC.
The region came under Roman control in the second century BC.
Muslims invaded mainland Portugal in the eighth century and were gradually expelled by the Christian Reconquista between 1238 and 1249 AD.
Portugal was initially recognized as a kingdom in 1179 and became a major maritime power during the Age of Discovery.
The Carnation Revolution in 1974 restored democracy in Portugal, ending the Portuguese Colonial War and leading to the independence of the last African territories.
Portugal is a member of the United Nations, the European Union, the Schengen Area, and the Council of Europe.
Portugal was one of the founding members of NATO, the eurozone, the OECD, and the Community of Portuguese Language Countries.
The name 'Portugal' originates from the Roman-Celtic place name 'Portus Cale', which is linked to the Latin word 'portus' meaning 'port'.
Portus Cale means 'Port of Cale' and was incorporated into the province of Gallaecia.
The name Portucale changed to Portugale in the 7th and 8th centuries and referred to the region between the Douro and Minho rivers by the 9th century.
Homo heidelbergensis entered the area of Portugal around 400,000 years ago, with the oldest human fossil being a 400,000-year-old skull found in the Cave of Aroeira.
Waves of Celts arrived in Portugal during the first millennium BC.
Celts intermarried with local populations in Portugal.
Formation of several different ethnic groups in Portugal due to Celtic migration.
Northern and central Portugal were dominated by a specific culture before the Roman conquest.
Southern Portugal had an older character believed to be non-Indo-European and likely related to Basque until Roman conquest.
Romans first invaded the Iberian Peninsula in 219 BC and annexed almost the entire peninsula during Julius Caesar's rule.
Lusitanians led by Viriathus controlled western Iberia.
Rome sent legions to suppress the rebellion but failed.
Viriathus was assassinated in 139 BC, and Tautalus succeeded him.
In 27 BC, Lusitania gained the status of Roman province.
Emperor Diocletian's reforms led to the separation of a northern province known as Gallaecia from Tarraconensis.
The Iberian Peninsula was occupied by Germanic tribes in 409 AD during the decline of the Roman Empire.
Suebian Kingdom established in Gallaecia with capital in Braga
Suebi and Visigoths were dominant Germanic tribes in modern Portugal
Suebian and Visigothic adoption of Catholicism from local inhabitants after initially following Arianism
Martin of Braga was an influential evangelist.
In 429, the Visigoths moved south to expel the Alans and Vandals.
The Visigoths founded a kingdom with its capital in Toledo.
In 585, Visigothic King Liuvigild conquered Braga and annexed Gallaecia.
The Iberian Peninsula was unified under the Visigothic Kingdom.
A new nobility class emerged during the Visigothic period, playing a key social and political role.
Councils of bishops created laws that elevated the clergy to a high-ranking class.
The Iberian Peninsula, including present-day Portugal, was invaded by the Umayyad Caliphate between 726 and 1249.
The Umayyad Caliphate conquered the Visigoths and began a rapid expansion in the Iberian Peninsula starting in 726.
The Emirate of Córdoba was established by Abd-ar-Rahman I after the west of the empire gained its independence.
The Emirate of Córdoba became the Caliphate of Córdoba in 929 and was dissolved in 1031 into 23 Taifa kingdoms.
The Almoravids conquered the Taifa kingdoms in 1086, followed by the Almohads in 1147.
Muslim population in Iberia consisted mainly of native Iberian converts to Islam and Berbers.
Arabs in Iberia were mainly noblemen from Syria and constituted a minority elite.
Viking incursions on the Iberian coast occurred between the 9th and 11th centuries, leading to small Norse settlements between Douro and Minho.
Nationalmuseet in Copenhagen
home-marks objects
Funen (Fyn), Denmark
Viking incursions along the Portuguese coast indicate historical interactions between Vikings and the region.
Pagan rituals practiced in a particular fishing region suggest cultural traditions that may have Norse roots.
The Reconquista was a period of Christian reconquest of the Iberian Peninsula from Moorish domination.
Pelagius of Asturias was elected leader in 718 and called for rebellion against the Moors.
Pelagius defeated the Moors in the Battle of Covadonga in 722, founding the Christian Kingdom of Asturias.
Vímara Peres was tasked by King Alfonso III of Asturias to reconquer and repopulate the region of Portugal, leading to the establishment of the County of Portugal.
Victory in the Battle of Ourique in 1139 led to the proclamation of Afonso Henriques as King of Portugal.
Afonso Henriques was recognized as the first king of Portugal in 1143 by King Alfonso VII of León and in 1179 by Pope Alexander III.
The Reconquista ended in 1249 with the capture of the Algarve and expulsion of the last Moorish settlements.
Portugal has one of the oldest established territorial borders in Europe.
Denis of Portugal signed the Treaty of Alcañices in 1297.
The Treaty of Alcañices established the border between the kingdoms of Portugal and Leon.
The first university in Portugal opened in Lisbon on March 1st, 1290, and was transferred to Coimbra in 1308.
Afonso IV succeeded Denis in 1325 and had a strained relationship with his father.
In 1373, Portugal made an alliance with England during the Fernandine Wars, which was reinforced by the Treaty of Windsor in 1386.
John of Aviz led a revolt against the Castilians.
John of Aviz became John I of Portugal.
The Battle of Aljubarrota resulted in a victory for John of Aviz and established the House of Aviz as the ruling house.
Fernão Lopes authored a history of Portugal during the new ruling dynasty.
Prince Henry the Navigator sponsored Portugal's exploration during the Age of Discovery.
The Treaty of Tordesillas of 1494 divided newly located lands outside Europe between Portugal and Spain.
Vasco da Gama became the first European to reach India by sea in 1498.
Pedro Álvares Cabral landed on Brazil and claimed it for Portugal in 1500.
The Treaty of Zaragoza, signed in 1529, divided the Pacific Ocean between Spain and Portugal.
The joining of the two crowns deprived Portugal of an independent foreign policy.
Portugal was involved in the Eighty Years' War between Spain and the Netherlands.
The Dutch Portuguese War (1595-1663) mainly involved Dutch companies invading Portuguese colonies.
John V's reign was marked by an influx of gold into the royal treasury from the royal fifth tax on precious metals from Portuguese colonies.
An estimated 600,000 Portuguese migrants moved to Colonial Brazil during the 18th-century gold rush.
Sebastião José de Carvalho e Melo was ennobled as the 1st Marquis of Pombal in 1738 after serving as the Portuguese Ambassador in London and Vienna.
Carvalho e Melo was entrusted with more control of the state by the King.
Carvalho e Melo became prime minister in 1755.
The King's confidence in Carvalho e Melo increased over time.
King José I banned the import of black slaves into mainland Portugal and India in 1761.
Marquis of Pombal supported the trafficking of slaves to Brazil through the creation of the *Companhia do Grão-Pará e Maranhão* and the *Companhia Geral de Pernambuco e Paraíba*.
King José I reorganised the army and navy and ended legal discrimination against different Christian sects.
The 1755 Lisbon earthquake occurred on 1 November 1755.
Magnitude of the earthquake is estimated to be between 8.5 and 9.0.
Casualties from the earthquake ranged from 12,000 to 50,000.
Marquis of Távora was tortured and executed in public alongside family members and servants.
Execution of the Marquis of Távora and his associates was characterized by extreme brutality.
The Jesuits were suppressed and expelled the year following the Távora affair.
Marquês de Pombal ruled Portugal from 1770 until Joseph I's death in 1777.
Queen Maria I of Portugal withdrew Pombal's political offices upon her accession to the throne and banished him to his estate, where he died in 1782.
During the Napoleonic invasions, the Portuguese royal family moved the court to Rio de Janeiro, making it the capital of the Portuguese empire between 1808 and 1821.
Pedro I of Brazil briefly became Pedro IV of Portugal.
Pedro abdicated the Portuguese crown in favor of his daughter, Maria da Glória.
The Liberal Wars, also known as the War of the Two Brothers or the Portuguese Civil War, resulted in Miguel's abdication and Maria II becoming Queen of Portugal.
Goa was a significant colony of Portuguese India where trade flourished.
Macau and Timor were subsidiary colonies of Portugal, with Macau located near Hong Kong and Timor north of Australia.
On 11 January 1890, the British government demanded the withdrawal of Portuguese forces from the area between Mozambique and Angola, which Portugal claimed as part of its Pink Map colonial project.
King Carlos I and Luís Filipe assassinated on 1 February 1908 by Republican and Carbonária members.
5 October 1910 coup d'état overthrew the Portuguese Monarchy and proclaimed the Republic.
António de Oliveira Salazar ruled Portugal between 1932 and 1968 within the Estado Novo regime.
Portugal was a founding member of NATO.
Portugal was a founding member of OECD.
Portugal joined the United Nations in 1955.
Portugal's economic development projects targeted Angola and Mozambique.
The actions aimed to affirm Portugal's status as a transcontinental nation rather than a colonial empire.
Dadra and Nagar Haveli separated from Portuguese rule in 1954, and the Republic of Dahomey annexed Fort of São João Baptista de Ajudá in 1961, marking the dissolution of the Portuguese Empire.
Portugal's armed conflict in Portuguese India against Indian Armed Forces
Defeat of Portuguese forces led to loss of remaining Portuguese territories in Indian subcontinent
Independence movements in Portuguese Angola, Portuguese Mozambique, and Portuguese Guinea triggered the Portuguese Colonial War (1961-1974)
Portugal experienced dissent and arms embargoes during the colonial war period.
Salazar governed Portugal until 1968 and was succeeded by Marcelo Caetano, leading to the short-lived Marcialist Spring.
The Carnation Revolution in April 1974 resulted in the independence of overseas territories and the restoration of democracy in Portugal.
Coup launched by extreme left-wing forces on 25 November
Counter-coup initiated by the military faction known as the Group of Nine
Group of Nine's victory prevented the establishment of a communist state and ended political instability
Mass exodus of Portuguese citizens from African territories following the retreat of overseas colonies.
Over one million Portuguese refugees fled the former Portuguese provinces in Africa.
Mário Soares became prime minister of Portugal after the Portuguese legislative election of 1976, serving from 1976 to 1978 and again from 1983 to 1985.
Portugal transitioned to democracy and alternated between socialism and neoliberalism.
Land reform and nationalizations were implemented in Portugal during this period.
The Portuguese Constitution was rewritten to incorporate socialist and communist principles before revisions in 1982 and 1989.
Portugal's economic situation after the revolution led to the pursuit of International Monetary Fund (IMF)-monitored stabilization programmes.
The first IMF-monitored stabilization programme in Portugal was from 1977 to 1978.
The second IMF-monitored stabilization programme in Portugal was from 1983 to 1985.
Portugal joined the European Economic Community in 1986.
European Structural and Investment Funds contributed to Portugal's economic progress.
Portugal gained easier access to foreign markets after joining the European Economic Community.
Macau was Portugal's last overseas territory, handed over to China in 1999.
East Timor's independence was formally recognised by Portugal in 2002.
East Timor is located in Asia.
Treaty of Lisbon entered into force on 1 December 2009
Treaty of Lisbon enhances efficiency and democratic legitimacy of the European Union
Country negotiated a loan with IMF and European Union in 2011 due to economic disruption from 2008 financial crisis
Portugal occupies an area on the Iberian Peninsula.
Portugal includes two archipelagos in the Atlantic Ocean: Madeira and the Azores.
Most Portuguese refer to the Iberian Peninsula as *the continent*.
The Tagus River flows from Spain, through continental Portugal, and into the Tagus Estuary at Lisbon before entering the Atlantic Ocean.
Portugal's landscape features mountainous regions in the north and rolling plains in the south, including the Algarve and Alentejo regions.
Mount Pico on Pico Island in the Azores is the highest peak in Portugal, and the Azores and Madeira archipelagos were formed by volcanic activity.
The exclusive economic zone of Portugal covers an area of 1,727,408 km2 (666,956 sq mi).
Portugal has the 3rd largest exclusive economic zone in the European Union and the 20th largest in the world.
Portugal's climate includes Mediterranean, temperate maritime, semi-arid, hot desert, and humid subtropical climates, with average temperatures varying significantly across regions.
Annual diurnal average temperatures in the Guadiana basin can reach high levels, with summer temperatures exceeding 40 °C (104 °F).
The record high temperature recorded in Amareleja is unspecified but exceeds 40 °C (104 °F).
Snowfalls occur regularly during winter in the interior North and Centre regions, especially in the highlands, with temperatures dropping below − °C ( °F) between October and May.
Continental Portugal receives around 2,300-3,200 hours of sunshine annually, averaging 4–6 hours in winter and 10–12 hours in summer.
The average sea surface temperature on the west coast of mainland Portugal ranges from 14–16 °C in January−March to 19–21 °C in August−October.
The Azores and Madeira have a subtropical climate, with variations between islands, and some Azorean islands classified as Mediterranean climate.
Rising temperatures and longer-lasting heat waves are effects of climate change in Portugal.
Portugal is home to six terrestrial ecoregions and is located in the Mediterranean Basin, a diverse hotspot of flora.
Around 20 percent of species in continental Portugal are exotic, contributing to biodiversity challenges.

Portugal experiences a seasonal upwelling system in the summer that brings cooler, nutrient-rich water to the sea surface, promoting phytoplankton and zooplankton growth and supporting rich marine biodiversity.
73% of freshwater fish in the Iberian Peninsula are endemic, which is the highest percentage of any region in Europe.
Portugal is a semi-presidential representative democratic republic with Lisbon as its capital, and Marcelo Rebelo de Sousa has been the President since 2016.

Presidential powers in Portugal include the appointment and dismissal of the Prime Minister, dissolving the Assembly, vetoing legislation, and declaring war with Government advice and Assembly authorization.
The Assembly of the Republic in Portugal is a single chamber parliament with a maximum of 230 deputies elected for a four-year term.
The current Prime Minister of Portugal is Luís Montenegro, and the Council of Ministers operates under the Prime Minister and includes Ministers and Secretaries of State with full executive powers.
Thirteen-member Constitutional Court oversees the constitutionality of laws in Portugal.
Portugal operates a multi-party system dominated by the Social Democratic Party and the Socialist Party.
Portugal is a member of the United Nations, NATO, OECD, and was a founding member of EFTA before joining the European Economic Community.
Portugal co-founded the Community of Portuguese Language Countries (CPLP) in 1996.
CPLP is also known as the Lusophone Commonwealth.
CPLP is an international organization and political association of nations where Portuguese is an official language.
First EU–Brazil summit hosted by Portugal in July 2007.
Second EU–African Union summit hosted by Portugal in December 2007.
NATO summit hosted by Portugal in November 2010.
Portugal has a friendship alliance and dual citizenship treaty with Brazil.
Portugal and the United Kingdom share the Anglo-Portuguese Alliance, signed in 1373.
Olivenza has been under continuous Spanish rule since the 19th century despite Portuguese claims since 1815.
Establishment year of 1364 by Italian mariners
Service under Prince Henry The Navigator
First noted by Portuguese navigator Diogo Gomes de Sintra in 1438
Portuguese ownership of the islands dates back to the 16th century until 1971 when the government purchased them to establish a natural reserve area.
The Selvagens Islands have been claimed by Spain since 1911, leading to political tensions between Portugal and Spain.
Portuguese Armed Forces consist of three branches: Navy, Army, and Air Force, serving primarily as a self-defence force.
The Portuguese military expenditure in 2023 was over 4 billion US dollars.
The Portuguese Army has 11,000 personnel organized into three brigades: an Infantry Brigade, a Mechanized Brigade, and a Rapid Reaction Brigade.
The Infantry Brigade is mainly equipped with Pandur II APC, M114 howitzer, and MIM-72 Chaparral air defence systems.
Navy consists of 7,000 personnel, including 900 marines
Navy is the world's oldest surviving naval force
Navy's fleet includes five frigates, two corvettes, two submarines, and 20 oceanic patrol vessels
National Republican Guard in Portugal
Security force subject to military law and organization
National Republican Guard comprises 25,000 personnel
Participation in international operations in Iraq
Participation in international operations in East Timor
United States military presence of 770 troops at Lajes Air Base, Terceira Island, Azores
Allied Joint Force Command Lisbon is one of the three main subdivisions of NATO's Allied Command Operations.
The Portuguese legal system is part of the civil law legal system.
Key laws in Portugal include the Constitution (1976), the Portuguese Civil Code (1966), and the Penal Code of Portugal (1982).
Supreme Court of Justice is the highest court in Portugal.
Constitutional Court is one of the supreme national courts in Portugal.
Public Ministry in Portugal is an independent body headed by the Attorney General of the Republic.
Portugal decriminalised all common drugs in 2001, allowing usage and personal possession.
Portugal became the sixth country in Europe to legalize same-sex marriage in 2010.
Euthanasia legalization has been proposed and blocked by the Constitutional Court, with the latest decision in 2025.
Portugal has 17 central prisons, 4 special prisons, 27 regional prisons, and 1 Support Detention Centre as of January 1, 2023.
The prison population of Portugal is approximately 12,257 inmates, and the incarceration rate has increased by 15% since 2010.
Portugal is divided into 308 municipalities and 3,092 civil parishes, with 18 districts in mainland Portugal and autonomous regions of the Azores and Madeira.
Portugal is a developed and high-income country with a GDP per capita of 82% of the EU27 average in 2024.
Portugal has the 13th largest gold reserve and the 8th largest proven reserves of lithium in the world.
Portugal has been a net beneficiary of the European Union budget since joining the EEC in 1986.
Banco de Portugal is the central bank of Portugal and part of the European System of Central Banks.
Portugal transitioned from a public consumption-based economic model to one focused on exports, private investment, and the high-tech sector since the 1990s.
In 2011, Portugal received a 78-billion-euro bailout from the European Union and the International Monetary Fund due to a severe recession.
Rents in Lisbon, Portugal, increased by 37% in 2022.
Inflation rate in Portugal reached 8% in 2022 and decreased to 5% in 2023.
Portugal's economic recovery from the COVID pandemic in 2022 was better than the EU average.
Portugal is the top producer of cork, accounting for about 50% of world production.
Portugal is the top producer of carob, accounting for about 30% of world production.
Portugal is among the world's top ten largest olive oil producers and the fourth largest exporter of olive oil.
In 2001, the gross agricultural product accounted for 4% of the economy in Portugal; in 2022, it was 2%.
As of 2023, tourism accounted for a significant portion of Portugal's GDP, contributing to nearly half of real GDP growth.
Portugal was elected *The Best European Country* by *USA Today* in 2014 and was recognized as *Europe's Leading Destination* in 2017 and *World's Leading Destination* in 2018 and 2019.
Authority responsible for funding and management of the system is the Ministry of Science, Technology and Higher Education.
Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia translates to Foundation for Science and Technology.
The system's oversight includes both the Ministry of Science, Technology and Higher Education and Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia.
Largest R&D units of public universities are characterized by high volumes of research grants and peer-reviewed publications.
Biosciences research institutions are included among the largest R&D units of public universities.
Instituto Gulbenkian de Ciência and Champalimaud Foundation are among the largest non-state-run research institutions, focusing on neuroscience and oncology.
International Iberian Nanotechnology Laboratory
Ibercivis distributed computing platform
Iberian bilateral state-supported research efforts
Portugal is a member of the European Space Agency (ESA).
Portugal is a member of the European Laboratory for Particle Physics (CERN).
Portugal is a member of ITER and the European Southern Observatory (ESO).
Lisbon Oceanarium is the largest aquarium in Europe.
Ciência Viva is a state agency focused on science-related exhibits and divulgation in Portugal.
National Museum of Natural History at the University of Lisbon is one of the notable science-related organizations in Portugal.
Portugal ranked 15th in the European Innovation Scoreboard 2011 for innovation expenditure and output.
Portugal ranked 31st in the Global Innovation Index in 2024.
Portugal has a road network of 68,732 km, including almost 3,000 km of motorways.
Vasco da Gama bridge is the longest bridge in the EU.
Vasco da Gama bridge is the second longest bridge in Europe.
Continental Portugal has four international airports located near Lisbon, Porto, Faro, and Beja.
Lisbon serves as a stopover for many foreign airlines.
TAP Air Portugal is the primary flag-carrier of Portugal.
The main airports in Portugal include those in Lisbon, Porto, Faro, Funchal (Madeira), and Ponta Delgada (Azores), managed by ANA – Aeroportos de Portugal.
Rail transport includes passenger and goods transport using 2,791 km of railway lines in service.
1,430 km of railway lines are electrified.
About 900 km of railway lines allow train speeds greater than 120 km/h (75 mph).
Lisbon Metro is part of the Lisbon metropolitan area subway systems.
Metro Sul do Tejo light rail system operates in the Lisbon metropolitan area with over 35 km of lines.
Porto Metro light metro system serves the Porto metropolitan area with more than 35 km of lines.
Lisbon tram services are supplied by Companhia de Carris de Ferro de Lisboa (Carris).
Carris has been operating tram services in Lisbon for over a century.
Porto's tram network began construction on 12 September 1895, marking a first for the Iberian Peninsula.
Portugal's total energy supply as of 2023 is comprised of 44% oil.
Portugal phased out coal-fired generation in 2021.
Portugal has been developing renewable energies including hydropower and wind power.
Moura Photovoltaic Power Station began operating in 2006 as the world's then largest solar power plant.
Aguçadoura Wave Farm opened in 2008 as the world's first commercial wave power farm.
Aguçadoura Wave Farm is located in the Norte region.
By 2006, 66% of the country's electrical production was from coal and fuel power plants.
In 2008, renewable energy resources produced 43% of the nation's electricity.
As of 2010, 70% of energy came from renewable sources, and electricity exports had outnumbered imports.
Portugal emitted around 339 million tonnes of greenhouse gases in 2023, approximately 5 tonnes per person, equating to about 1% of global total emissions.
Portugal is part of the EU's plan to reduce emissions by a minimum of 55% by 2030 compared to 1990 levels and is committed to carbon neutrality and net zero by 2050.
As of December 31, 2023, the estimated population of Portugal was 10,639,726.
Portugal has experienced a sub-replacement fertility rate since the 1980s, with an estimated total fertility rate as of 2024 that is among the lowest in the world.
As of 2024, Portugal has the world's 4th highest number of citizens over 65 years in the population, indicating an ageing population.
In 2023, 12% of Portugal's population consisted of legal resident foreigners, totaling 1,293,463 individuals.
340,000 resident foreigners acquired Portuguese citizenship between 2008 and 2022.
In 2022, almost 21,000 foreign residents obtained Portuguese citizenship (11,170 female, 9,674 male).
Portugal experienced an influx of immigrants from various regions, including Ukraine, Brazil, and African countries, leading to a significant net immigration.
Significant numbers of expatriates come from Eastern Europe, including Moldova, Ukraine, Romania, and Brazil.
A significant number of EU citizens residing in the country originate from Italy, France, Germany, and other northern European countries.
The British expatriate community is primarily made up of retired pensioners living in the Algarve and Madeira.
The National Statistics Institute (INE) conducted a study between 2022 and 2023.
13% of the population have an immigrant background, amounting to a million people.
947,500 individuals are first generation immigrants, mainly located in the Lisbon metropolitan area and the Algarve.
Roman Catholicism is the dominant religion in Portugal.
Portugal has no official religion, but the Catholic Church has historically been the state religion.
The 2021 Census indicates that a significant portion of the Portuguese population identifies as Roman Catholic Christian.
Influence of African Traditional Religion in Traditional African Herbal Medicine
Influence of Chinese Traditional Religion in Traditional Chinese Medicine
Connection between traditional religions and herbal medicine practices
Portugal is a secular state with church and state separation established during the First Portuguese Republic and reiterated in the 1976 Portuguese Constitution.
Portuguese is the official language of Portugal, with Mirandese recognized as a co-official regional language and Barranquenho dialect formally recognized since 2021.
The educational system in Portugal consists of preschool, basic education (nine years compulsory), secondary education (three years compulsory since 2010), and higher education.
Portuguese higher education institutions commonly use faculties, institutes, and schools as subdivisions.
In 2021, Portugal's adult literacy rate was noted, though the specific percentage is not provided.
According to the PISA 2018 assessment, Portugal scored around the OECD average in reading, mathematics, and science.
Portuguese universities have existed since 1290.
The oldest Portuguese university was first established in Lisbon before moving to Coimbra.
The University of Lisbon is the largest university in Portugal.
Higher education in state-run educational establishments is provided on a competitive basis.
A system of numerus clausus is enforced using a national database on student admissions.

Higher education institutions offer additional vacant places through extraordinary admission processes for specific groups such as sportsmen, mature applicants over 23, international students, and others.
Portugal has entered into cooperation agreements with the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and other US institutions.
In 2025, Portugal ranked as the 23rd best healthcare system in the world.
Portugal's healthcare system ranking was previously 12th place in the 2000 World Health Organisation ranking.
The health system consists of the National Health Service (SNS), special social health insurance schemes, and voluntary private health insurance.
The SNS provides universal coverage, while 55% of the population is covered by health subsystems, 43% by private insurance, and 12% by mutual funds.
The Ministry of Health manages the SNS and develops health policy, with five regional health administrations responsible for implementing national policy objectives.
Employer contributions are a main funding source for health subsystems.
Employee contributions fund health subsystems alongside employer contributions.
Direct payments by patients and voluntary health insurance premiums contribute significantly to funding health subsystems.
Approximately 30,000 deaths per year in Portugal are attributed to cardiovascular diseases (CVD), accounting for one third of all annual deaths.
Cerebrovascular disease is the leading cause of death in Portugal, with over 11,000 deaths annually, contrasting with trends seen in Eur-A countries.
Oncological diseases make up 22% of all deaths in Portugal, with more frequent cases of cervical and prostate cancer and a decrease in diabetes mortality rates from 2010 to 2021.
Portugal modernised public cultural facilities in the 1990s and 2000s, including the Belém Cultural Centre, Serralves Foundation, and Casa da Música.
Portugal is home to 17 UNESCO World Heritage Sites, ranking 9th in Europe and 18th in the world.

Traditional Portuguese architecture includes Manueline style and Pombaline style, with modern interpretations like Soft Portuguese style and renowned architects such as Eduardo Souto de Moura and Álvaro Siza Vieira.
Tomás Taveira is notable for stadium design in Portugal.
Dry cod, known as bacalhau, is a significant ingredient in Portuguese cuisine, with 365 different ways to prepare it.
Portuguese wines include Vinho Verde, Alvarinho, Vinho do Douro, and sweet varieties like Port Wine and Madeira Wine.
Carlos Botelho and Almada Negreiros are modernist painters/writers.
Almada Negreiros was a friend of poet Fernando Pessoa and painted his portrait.
Portuguese literature, one of the earliest Western literatures, developed through text and song.
D. Fernando (1367–1383) sponsored Pêro Menino in writing *Livro da Falcoaria*.
Luís de Camões wrote the epic poem *Os Lusíadas* influenced by Virgil's *Aeneid*.
José Saramago, a prominent Portuguese author, won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1998.
Queima das Fitas is a student festival in Portugal showcasing established and emerging musicians.
Portugal won the Eurovision Song Contest 2017 with the song 'Amar pelos dois' by Salvador Sobral.
The golden period of Portuguese music is associated with classical polyphony in the 17th century.
Artur Pizarro is a notable pianist.
Maria João Pires is a distinguished pianist.
Carlos Damas is a prominent violinist.
Fundação Gulbenkian is one of the most important symphony orchestras in Portugal.
Porto National Orchestra is a key symphony orchestra in Portugal.
Teatro Nacional de São Carlos in Lisbon is the most representative opera house in Portugal.
Eusébio, Figo, and Cristiano Ronaldo are symbols of Portuguese football history.
José Mourinho and Abel Ferreira are renowned Portuguese football managers.
The Portugal national football team won the UEFA Euro 2016 and finished first in the 2018–19 UEFA Nations League.
Portuguese sports clubs, including the big three, compete in multiple sports such as roller hockey, basketball, futsal, handball, volleyball, and athletics.
The Portuguese national rugby union team qualified for the 2027 Rugby World Cup.
The Portuguese national rugby sevens team has participated in the World Rugby Sevens Series.
Volta a Portugal is the most important road cycling race in Portugal.
Major professional cycling teams in Portugal include Sporting CP, Boavista, Clube de Ciclismo de Tavira, and União Ciclista da Maia.
Portugal hosts the MEO Rip Curl Pro Portugal, a stage of the World Surf League Championship Tour at Supertubos in Peniche.
Portugal is a leading golf destination in the world.
ISBN 978-0-521-60397-3 is a unique identifier for books.
The book "Colonialism: An International, Social, Cultural, and Political Encyclopedia" is authored by Melvin Eugene Page and Penny M. Sonnenburg.
The publication year of the encyclopedia is 2003.
Constituição da República Portuguesa refers to the Constitution of the Portuguese Republic.
The VI Revisão Constitucional edition was published in 2004.
Wikimedia Commons and Wikivoyage provide media and travel information related to Portugal.

Official Portuguese Government website in English and Portuguese
Portugal is a member state of NATO, the European Union, and the Community of Portuguese Language Countries.
